properties.description: An item familiar to every Maen Academy student - the resonance created by precious metals makes it easier to overcome the hurdles of learning magic.
A graduate's cloak speaks far louder of their years at the Academy than any written testimony ever could.

The infamous Beast of Ratten terrorized the countryside for several years, slaughtering at least a hundred livestock and a couple dozen peasants. Fortunately, it was tracked down and slain by Sir Albrecht - may the Host bless his brave soul.
The mastery of metalworking techniques was one of the ancient Alds' greatest strengths - some of the miraculously surviving relics from their time continue to inspire awe even today.
Has enough power to knock a charging knight from his saddle.
Aldwynn smiths are known for two things: crafting the heaviest and the most durable armor in Aldor and forging arms that can chop through it.

A while ago, His Lordship Brodwyn der Razen made it his personal mission to fit his soldiers with crossbows of this design. His success inspired some other prominent Council commanders to eventually do the same.
With these on your feet, you feel like you can hold your ground against a small army.
It must have arrived here from either Fjall or Skadia, the lands famous for their amber trade.
A legacy of an age when the skill and artistry of the dwarven smiths were the envy of the entire Continent.
The metal aged so poorly that it's become impossible to tell the ring's age: could be a couple hundred years, could be a thousand.
Despite being at least half a millennia old, it somehow continues to perfectly hold the edge.
Fashioned in either the Ald or Firn tradition - the splendor of the adornments suggests they were once worn by a prominent chieftain or high priest.
Barely forms any wrinkles when subjected to portal jumps.
A distinct mantle of the Circle of Arcanistics, thoroughly imbued with pure magic essence.
Don't even try prying the enchanted amethyst out of this staff's headpiece - that is unless you want to spend the rest of your life outside of this plane of existence.
The design was clearly inspired by the styles of the Bronze Isles - Arno di Berro himself wears a similar-looking belt.
From the stitching to the buckles, every single part is crafted from the finest materials and with flawless workmanship.
There are letters painted along the rim - words of prayer, perhaps, or a noble family's motto.
Staff duels are a staple of fencers' training, with some fighters growing so confident in their prowess that they don't shy away from bringing the weapon into real combat.
It might soften a club blow. Can't expect more from it.
The surface of the blade bears an inscription, faded by time. The words are no longer decipherable, but Arna can still recite them from memory.
One of King Etbert's decrees prohibited his vassals from quarreling with one another. Therefore, most conflicts began involving middlemen - assassins for hire, for instance.
Might come in handy if you ever need to hide from arrows raining down from the walls of a besieged castle.
Astromancers claim such staves to possess an astonishingly precise ability to model the motion of our... What do they call it? Right, our "planet".
A distant cousin of a goedendag, forged by a skilled weaponsmith.
Hundreds of years ago, this boar-shaped piece was carried at the head of countless battle-hardened phalanxes.
An intricately embroidered cape cut from strange azure fabric. By the looks of its ornamented gold fibula, it was brought to Aldor from the Jacinth Kingdoms.
Barbutes offer a great balance between protection and unobtrusiveness, which makes them well-suited for archers and light infantry.

For the longest time, Aldorian nobles had been rather dismissive towards axes. Their perspective took a turn when the late King Etbert introduced a fashion for richly decorated axes, turning them into a symbol of power.
The Council's nobles expected a swift and decisive victory - and miscalculated. As a result, baronial weaponry is now being passed down from one new owner to the next.
Baselards are so popular among both the nobility and townsmen that they are often viewed as an accessory rather than an actual weapon.
A simple butcher's cleaver, crudely refashioned into a sword-like shape by an unknown craftsman.
A stable stance during spellcasting matters just as much as the precision of gestures - and quality boots provide welcome support.
Battlemages aren't averse to risks. Many of them willingly forgo proper head protection to stay unhindered while casting complex spells.
Everything about it speaks of practicality and ease of wear: thick fabric, muted colors, simple - albeit golden - fastenings...
Its inner layer is embroidered with protective spells.
Much lighter than a common gambeson and much sturdier than a mantle, this chestpiece combines the best of both worlds.

Well-trained and experienced mages are a force to be reckoned with, which makes them a prime target on the battlefield. Unsurprisingly, they're always on a lookout for quality armor that doesn't compromise the freedom of movement.
Battle-proven mages are always in demand and, consequently, are wealthy enough to afford custom-tailored, hard-wearing mantles.
Every so often, there's simply no time for casting spells, and fighting up close becomes the only option.
Lends itself to a higher grip whenever the enemy gets too close for a full swing.
In the eyes of the Fjall dwarves, the sling is a far more honorable weapon than the bow - so much so that even battle-hardened veterans readily wield it in combat.
Named after forge bellows, this helmet's visor has numerous perforations, allowing for less restricted breathing and improved air flow.
Named after forge bellows, this helmet's visor has numerous perforations, allowing for less restricted breathing and improved air flow.
Rather than relying on your arm strength alone, the belt hook also allows to engage back muscles when drawing the string, which makes reloading this crossbow noticeably less tiring.
Fjall berserkers draw strength not only from the Ancestors' protection but also from the spirit of their guardian beast.
Its previous owner could equally likely be an orc or a dwarf.
It's almost asking to be put to work.
Whether they're making armor, blades, or horseshoes, no smith would consider reaching for tongs or hammer before putting on a good pair of mittens.
What need is there for a helmet, when in an honest duel no blade could hope to reach your head?
With a proper polish, they could easily pass for a mirror.
Lightweight, resilient, and cut for mobility - despite the many metal plates sewn into the lining.
Expert fencers can afford to skimp on heavy hand protection, trusting in their ability to parry every blow.
Blademasters are paid double wages to fight on the frontline - and they don't need to be asked twice to demonstrate why this expense is well-founded.
An old-fashioned crossbow, restored with care, if somewhat hastily. The opening words of a common Hieronite prayer are carved into its stock.
This homemade shield is just a few rough planks cobbled together. Still better than nothing.
A weapon of choice among the corsairs of the Bronze archipelago, utterly indispensable during boardings.
As gamblers like to say, always keep an ace up your sleeve and a dagger down your boot.

Few corners of Aldor are as perilous as the Northern Borderlands, where the reach of raiders and monsters from Fjall is held in check only by the preparedness of the local garrisons, whose gear could inspire envy in even some noble lords.
Criminals and cutthroats with a handsome price on their heads are dangerous quarry, so hunting them calls for every precaution and defense one can muster.
The belief that a weapon-shaped talisman helps mastering its full-sized version has been commonplace since the time of ancient Firns.
After the Maafs conquered Jarimad, the nomad chieftains presented their bozdogans to the conquerors - who, unexpectedly, took quite a liking to the weapon.
Has a remarkable tendency to tie itself in impossible knots.
A brandistock is a fairly uncommon weapon which is sometimes issued to sergeants of the Road Guard.
A typical coming-of-age gift for a merchant's son.
Brass has been around for centuries - the technique used to make this ring is rooted in the Firn metal-working tradition.
A handy tool for sealing personal letters and business papers.
These swords are forged by the dozen in underground smithies, far from prying eyes and unwanted questions.
Offers a bit of respite from the scorching midday sun.
It's best to keep your distance when someone is swinging this axe around - it chops limbs and heads with equal ease.
For a noblewoman, it's a boring ring with a cheap gem. For a poor commoner's daughter - an incredible luxury and her greatest treasure.
Your main ally in the never-ending struggle for tidiness and order.

Among the brotherhoods created after the dissolution of the Triune Hand Order, the Brotherhood of Ouroboros is the most secretive and mysterious one. Its symbol has a plethora of interpretations: the most popular one is a righteous death followed by a rebirth.

The Brotherhood of the Asp's knights are best known for abandoning Aldor to settle in the mountains of Ahjat. According to legends, their hidden citadel stands as a sanctuary of learning at the crossroads of religion, magic, and alchemy - concealed from prying eyes and protected by the Sultan himself.
The Falcon Company is one of the most infamous detachments of the Magistrate's army. Their crossbows were fashioned by master craftsmen of Brynn in the likeness of elven bolt throwers.
A weapon of rank, proudly carried by captains of the Brynn garrison. The way they strut about with these axes on their belts is a sight to behold.
These shields are employed by famous Crossbow Brigades, the elite units of the Brynn garrison.
A line of soldiers bristling with such polehammers is a force that can threaten even the heavy cavalry. Especially the heavy cavalry.
The design has unmistakably Brynnian roots, blending a multi-pronged hammerhead with a sharp spike, typical of the South.
Any duelist worth their salt must possess three things: a buckler, a trusty blade, and a carefully rehearsed Brynn accent.
So withered that it's almost impossible to think of any good use for it.
A reinforced variant of a barbute, preferred by those who don't find scars attractive.
A dozing sentry will snap to attention at the sound of their heavy tread.
Fashioned to keep the wearer in one piece, letting them live through scrapes they have no business surviving.

It's not unusual for leaders of the most celebrated mercenary warbands to amass fortunes that eclipse those of many aristocrats. Naturally, much of it is spent on acquiring the best equipment available.
Anyone who lives long enough to earn the rank of captain knows full well how to avoid putting themselves into unnecessary danger - sometimes you just don't need more than a gambeson.
Built for the battlefield, and equally useful for loudly slamming a fist on the war table when the campaign isn't going to plan.
For a mercenary warband's captain, a greatsword isn't just a weapon but a proof of their rank and combat experience. And if they need to spend some extra gold to make it stand out, then so be it.
It strikes a perfect balance between aesthetics, battlefield performance, and comfort of use - such swords are often carried by captains of the most renowned mercenary companies.
A captain's poleaxe stands out even at a distance - a rallying point, and a reassuring sign that the company is still in the fight.
Will hold a scabbard, a supply pouch, a key to the war chest, a map case, a list of passwords - and whatever else the situation demands.
In the hands of mercenary captains, warhammers are mainly treated as a symbol of command. Nevertheless, this doesn't prevent them from being highly effective tools of war.
An ordinary hammer that can also be used to pull out bent or errant nails.
Any weapon has a potential to be transformed into an object of art. It's the age of redundancy after all.
It's amazing how much a plain gold ring can transform in the hands of a skilled craftsman.
It was most likely crafted for a wealthy Royal Ranger, who had been granted a fief for his loyal service.
Such necklaces are an essential attribute of prosperous city aristocrats, who never miss a chance to put their wealth on display.

The Castelliers were the Bronzian branch of the Order of the Triune Hand, famed for their relentless fight against piracy and the construction of impregnable fortresses, from which they took their name.

Judging by the engraving, it once belonged to Sir der Ulne, a famous glutton and a drunkard. A dozen years ago, during one of his feasts, Sir der Ulne met a heroic death in a prolonged battle with a whole roast hog.
A true piece of art - many weapon connoisseurs would pay through the nose to acquire it for their collection.

The famed Brynnian weaponsmith, Etien Morel, crafted many masterpieces in his lifetime, and this elaborate halberd is one of them. Tragically, his life was cut short when he perished during the siege of Brynn, burning alive along with his workshop.
The gilded ornaments seem to be the craftsman's attempt at adapting the traditional Radiant Wars motifs to fit the shape of a modern helmet.
The gilded ornaments seem to be the craftsman's attempt at adapting the traditional Radiant Wars motifs to fit the shape of a modern helmet.
Small, light, and, quite importantly, cheap - a helmet worn by many guards and militia.
Judging by the eye-catching band and the pair of metal discs added for extra protection, this helmet likely belonged to a merchant's son during his service in the town militia.
A chain is a weapon of street and tavern fights, hardly suitable for real combat.
If you don't have a waist to speak of, it's not wise to wear this belt over a civil garment - the links have a notable tendency to pinch and chafe.
Skadian warhammers are forged to last and are sought after even outside of their country of origin.
The Chervenian nobility is small but proud. Centuries of rivalry with the rest of Nistra's aristocracy have driven them to cultivate a unique style in dress and arms, ensuring they always stand out.
With crossbows like these, a citadel garrison can tie down an entire army for months on end.
Initially these swords were used only by the nobility of the Firn foothills, but now they can be found all over Aldor.
The high collar and layered padding should be more than enough to keep the wearer from spitting their teeth after taking a club to the face.
An ancient, preferably Axonian coin with a hole drilled in it is a common charm for better luck.
Swords are prohibitively expensive for most commoners, while axes and clubs can't be comfortably tucked into a belt. What else is left to an honest peasant?
Helmets of this shape have been in use for centuries, commonly worn by those who can't afford anything better.
In Aldor, a copper chain is as much an accessory as it is a protective charm.
The cheapest jewelry money can buy. It's said that wearing copper is beneficial to one's constitution.
A solid choice when fighting on foot. This particular shield bears the colors of the Royal Council.
It's hard to imagine a more wasteful and extravagant way to belt one's clothes.
Stylish, light, durable, and unbelievably comfortable, a dream come true for most mages.
Court mages have a very particular taste in clothes. Their goal is to dazzle and leave an impression, so they gravitate towards the craziest styles and colors.
Just a few years worth of village taxes is all it costs for a lord to adorn his court mage with one of these diadems.
Gems and noble metals are treasured for their ability to resonate with magic. What at first may appear as a display of unrestrained opulence can in fact be a tool of the trade.

Mages who find themself a part of a noble court are a subject of envy. The life they lead is comfy, lucrative, and devoid of any real dangers - that is until their master decides to participate in a military campaign...
This enchanted staff's headpiece was designed for conducting all types of magic.

A cranequin is a cunning mechanism which made crossbows even more deadly than they had already been. At least a dozen engineers and master craftsmen from all over Aldor compete for the title of its original inventor.
Stars and celestial bodies are held in superstitious reverence in Ahjat, as their positions in the sky govern the outcome of rituals performed by Hazzun oracles.
A tribute to the principle that offense is the finest form of defense.
These plain-looking trinkets are popular among the peasantry - supposedly, the hole in the middle can ward off the evil eye.
A commendable, even if not particularly successful, attempt at forging a real longsword.
Keeps your mind cold and your ears warm.
Made of deceptively light cloth, this mantle is fortified with cryomantic enchantments that can withstand even the harshest of frosts.
Cryomancers love to joke about this staff's bone-chilling properties. The last time anyone laughed at this pun was a century ago.
A crude club for a crude peasant.
This bow's curved shape allows it to send arrows further and with more precision than usual, though it has a bit of a kick which takes time to get used to.
This cuirass holds sentimental value to Arna - it used to be the most treasured heirloom of the House der Vyrne, passed down through generations. Time wasn't kind to it.
The very name of this dagger sounds like someone is choking on their own blood.
The sheer number of notches can be a source of either pride or dread. Depends on who does the tallying.
Dirwin has been wearing this vest for so long, it's a miracle it hasn't yet fused with him.
The doloire is also known as a "wagoner's axe". It's often carried by caravaneers tasked with escorting military wagons.
Despite its formidable appearance, this axe is rather unwieldy in a real fight.
The roads are dangerous, the turns unpredictable - the only thing worth trusting is your own blade.

The Elder Prince's druzhyna doesn't give ground even in the face of insurmountable odds. It's most distinguished members may receive a great privilege - a gift of an honorary weapon granted personally by the Prince.
The seal on the pommel leaves no room for interpretation: this exotic dagger used to belong to Orro Denye, the one and only Duke of Bronza.
Single combat with two-handed swords is a form of art, where your reflexes and precision matter just as much as brute strength.
A little something to complete the image of a daring fighter and a relentless heartbreaker.
In addition to the fighting skill, duelists also continuously sharpen their fashion sense.
An effective safeguard against treacherous thrusts and jabs at vital areas.
Some duelists become so apt at wielding a dagger that even sword strikes are no challenge for them to parry and counter.
A fair number of duelists prefer wearing all black when showing up for a fight. Why? Blood is harder to spot on dark fabric.
Career duelists in particular are fond of this style of gloves - they look especially dashing wrapped around a sword hilt.
If there's anything that dwarves appreciate more than booze and looting, it's a good axe.
A customary headwear of Fjall seidrs, practitioners of the bizarre mix of Axonian magic and local pagan rituals.
Dwarves have a preference for round shields - they are suitable for tight formations as well as one-on-one combat.
The headpiece in the form of a sea leviathan is carved with such attention to detail that it looks like it could start spewing flames at any moment.

When the Aldorian army was routed by the Nistrian cavalry at the battle of Thorny Hill, the Eeders militia took the humiliating defeat to heart and started attaching special anti-cavalry hooks to their axes.
The production process for these famous longbows isn't exactly common knowledge - the denizens of Eeders tend to keep to themselves.
Novice electromancers often complain about being constantly distracted by the constant electric crackling produced by these cowls. They quickly grow accustomed to it though.
Barely touching the material sends a sharp, tingling sensation up one's arm. That is unless you're an electromancer.

The Pyromancy Circle used to spread malicious rumors that the death of Frid Konnoken, the founder of the Electromancy Circle, was caused by him foolishly allowing a similar staff to come in contact with a street puddle.
In Jacinth Kingdoms, felt boots are sported by people from all walks of life, be it Ohirian nomads, maafs of Ahjat, or ghazi of Jibey.
Elven flails serve mostly a ceremonial purpose, carried only by the most skillful of warriors to flaunt their status. No money is spared to make these weapons look the part.
There is a faint flowery scent coming from the cloth. It reminds you of places you've never had a chance to travel to.

Although Eraffa is the dominant faith in the Jacinth Kingdoms, the influence of sun worship remains evident even after many centuries. Various solar motifs frequently appear in elven-made trinkets and adornments - this belt's buckle is as fine an example as any.

Sipahi are the heavy cavalry that forms the core of the Ahjat Empire's army, a cornerstone of its many victories. Unsurprisingly, their gear is crafted only by the best armorsmiths on the personal orders of Ahjat emirs.
Longaxes are exotic to elven lands - the peoples of Jacinth who use them can be counted on the fingers of one hand.
Some of the local aristocrats favor eastern sabers over traditional Aldorian swords - despite any weird looks they get from their subjects.
Elves call this hammer shape a "bull horn" for the distinctive spike on its back end.
Perfect for displaying one's fashion sense during a drinking night.

The apparent unity of Jibey, Ahjat, and other realms of Jacinth is merely a mirage. In reality, each is a patchwork of diverse tribes, khanates, and emirates, with every local "monarch" wielding almost absolute power over their people.
It would be a mistake to perceive the gilding as a mere decoration. To a battlemage, gold rims and edges can actually be of great practical use.

Despite the strict isolation, some goods still get smuggled from the Bronze Isles to Aldor. A lot of the time the contraband includes various swords: if anything, the islanders have had a lot of practice crafting weaponry.

The sun-worshiping cult of the Everflaming was outlawed by the grandfather of the current Amir of Jibey. Only a few isolated communes now remain of what was once a popular faith, having fled persecution to the distant fringes of the empire.
A longaxe is a highly unusual choice for a ceremonial weapon. And by the looks of the decorations, the original owner shelled out a pretty sum for it.
Don't let the glint of gold mislead you: despite its elegant ornaments, this polemace shatters skulls and bones just as efficiently as its plain counterparts.
From a certain distance, the masterfully cut ruby can easily be mistaken for a drop of fresh blood.
A tabar is a symbol of maafs, the warrior estate which holds the most power in the Ahjat Empire. Even the nobility require a special permission to arm themselves with one.
The handle makes this sword's origins crystal clear - Brynn weaponsmiths have a soft spot for elegant and somewhat ostentatious detailing.
There was a time in Aldor when hunting birds became an item of prestige. A lord without a personal falconeer would feel inadequate even in front of his own court.
A pair of expertly-crafted boots that will add a spring to your step while promenading the streets.
A time-honored charm meant to bring luck during a hunt.

The multiple attempts to marry Hieronite teachings with elven rituals produced a number of prominent Aldorian saints capable of glimpsing into the future - nevertheless, most of the clergy rejected this path, branding it as a perversion of the church traditions.
When it comes to inventing weapons for killing their own kind, the creativity of people never fails to achieve new heights.
More than a few Aldorian folk songs were shaped to the rhythm of this axe.
A garment beloved by the aristocrats who spend all their free time on mastering the sword: it's tough, boasts strong seams, and looks fancy to boot.
Rich peasants like to look the part - after all, why not wear better clothes if you have the funds?
The Firns had a reputation not only as excellent marksmen, but also as gifted bowyers: their bow designs still remain in common use.
A short-lived fad had weaponsmiths in each major Aldorian city competing fiercely to create the most extravagant warhammer. This one was clearly among the winners.
Fist shields don't get in the way and allow to safely parry weaker attacks.
Initially crafted in jest, this hammer shape quickly caught on and is no longer treated as an outlandish curiosity.
Fjall iron is hardly of the best quality, yet dwarves have an incredible knack for forging relatively decent armor from it.
What it lacks in physical defenses, it makes up for with runesingers' enchantments.
When flagbearers are slain in battle, their comrades are usually quick to follow suit - providing the former with quality armor is plain common sense.
A flamberge's wavy blade deals such dreadful wounds, that the voices for prohibiting it outright grow louder with each passing day.
What's the main advantage of flanged maces? They don't get stuck when they crush skulls and plate armor.
Its colors are reminiscent of the cowls worn by students of the Academy.
In spellcasting, much hinges on dexterity and nimble fingers - which is why light bracers and thin cloth gloves are the preferred handwear of most sorcerers.

A magically gifted person can try their luck as an apprentice to an experienced sorcerer. They rarely turn anyone down - not for the love of teaching, but largely because there's always a use for a personal servant.
Light enough to swing with relative ease while still carrying enough weight to hack through armor and shields.
Seasoned soldiers expect to be provided with better weapons, usually purchased with the gold directly from their lord's coffers.
A military-style belt - these are often crafted on the request of soldiers and mercenaries.
A favorite chestpiece of the Aldorian light infantry - it protects much better than a simple gambeson while being less cumbersome than plate armor.
Has the potential for many surprises - whether they harm or help the opponent is up to the wielder's skill.
A halberd is beloved by the Aldorian infantry for its versatility. Being able to use your weapon for both slashing and thrusting attacks is a valuable asset on the battlefield.
While many will deny it, Aldorians actually learned a few things from dwarves - how to make such axes, as an example.
These swords can improve your chances when fighting a heavily armored opponent, making them a popular choice among common foot soldiers.
Hauberks protect well and don't restrict mobility. On the downside, you can't make one without thousands of metal rings and lots and lots of time - obviously, all of that adds to the price.
A long, sturdy shield. Its rims are reinforced with iron bands.
Its main purpose is to halt the enemy cavalry in its tracks, and it does so with flying colors.
Most suitable for a fight in tight formation.
Has the capacity to injure even a heavily armored foe, but there are far better options if your opponent is light on defenses.
It hails from the area around Forholt, where it was once carried by mounted guards. Contrary to its name, this weapon has more in common with a bardiche than a staff.
Prosperous landholding peasants make up the backbone of the Aldorian militia, arriving at their lord's summons equipped with their own arms and armor.
It's trimmed with luscious fur of some unknown Fjall beast.
It won't protect you from anything but cold. On the other hand, Fjall dwarves don't usually need more than that.
Makes you look fat, but who cares about appearances in the thick of a fight?
These feathered hats are a distinguishing mark of the gamekeepers at the service of local lords.
These crossbows are excellent at taking out siege engineers even when shooting over large distances
A heavier version of an eederax: the traditional hook became a part of the axeblade itself.
Pulling up this cowl makes you feel inexplicable unity with the ground under your feet.
The runic embroidery strengthens the connection between a geomancer and the boulders they summon.
A staff fashioned from nigh indestructible stone, yet somehow it still weighs lighter than wood.
Ghazi are warriors of the Jacinth Kingdoms who voluntarily joined the holy war against King Etbert's invasion. Naturally, their ranks included many highborn elves...
A successful attempt at making an axe look luxurious without sacrificing its battle performance.
A belt made of durable, high-quality leather in accordance with the latest Brynn fashion trends. Its buckle proudly displays some nobleman's monogram.
Hardly the most elegant piece of jewelry - the craftsman was clearly more interested in the practical applications of gold than its aesthetics.
No need to spend countless crowns to convince the onlookers of your great wealth: an elegant gilded dagger on your hip will speak for itself.
Trimmed with velvet and tastefully accented with gold - the sort of gauntlets worn while signing declarations of war and accepting surrenders.
A distinguished staff for a distinguished mage: with gilding this lavish, there is plenty of precious metal for magic to course through.
Even though it bears a resemblance to a spear, thrusting with a glaive is not very effective - it's basically a broad sword attached to a shaft.
To the knightly estate's great displeasure, this modest peasant weapon is perfectly capable of slipping through gaps in even the heaviest armor.
Aquamarine favors the bold and the courageous: reputedly, it brings good luck in risky endeavors.
Diamonds this monstrous can usually only be found in royal crowns.
Apart from the Academy Circles' archmages, there aren't that many people who have the crowns for this kind of jewelry.
Gold is an excellent magical conduit. Maybe that's where its value comes from?
Signet rings like this one are beloved by bigwigs of the Grand Magistrate.
Set with a masterfully cut sapphire. The dream of every sorcerer.

Niran of Maen spent many years of his life collecting and restoring the scattered knowledge about the magical properties displayed by various gems. His efforts were instrumental to making sapphire what it is today, a stone strongly associated with the practitioners of magic.
A plain medallion without much flair - its value comes exclusively from the material.
The Grandmaster was the highest rank in the now-disbanded Order of the Triune Hand, yet their flails of office bore almost no adornments, honoring the vow of austerity.
A solid choice when fighting on foot. This particular shield bears the colors of the Gray Army.
Technically not a sword but a very large knife - though who besides weapon collectors would lose sleep over such a distinction?
The beak-like visor of this sallet keeps the wearer's face safe from most dangers. In general, such helmets are worn by warriors of noble blood.
The beak-like visor of this sallet keeps the wearer's face safe from most dangers. In general, such helmets are worn by warriors of noble blood.
Commonly issued to guards of higher rank.
A perfect example of a weapon's effectiveness relying entirely on the user's skill: this bow is pretty much useless in the hands of a novice archer.

Village guards are usually recruited from peasantry, where most are already familiar with a bow. As for town guards, it takes much less effort to arm them with crossbows than teach them archery from scratch.
These halberds are typically forged from poor steel: city guards don't see a lot of action.

When King Etbert ascended the throne, Aldor was drowning in crime and infighting. His swift success in regaining control over the brigand-infested roads can mostly be attributed to the creation of guard units that answered directly to him rather than local lords.
These swords were intended for King Etbert's bodyguards. After the monarch's death, at least several of them ended up stolen from the royal arsenal.

Not too long before his demise, King Etbert ordered to form a unit of crossbow-wielding guardsmen. The most skilled Aldwynn bowyers were tasked with providing the weaponry, but they'd managed to produce only a few dozen crossbows before the monarch's death.
The cuffs are embossed with King Etbert's crest, leaving no question about the original owner's allegiance.
After the Royal Guard was disbanded, many former guardsmen decided to try their luck as mercenaries. Rather predictably, they took all their equipment with them, these shields included.
Making these swords is a routine job for the Guild's smiths - day after day, night after night...
It's said that Aldorian smiths got the idea of forging such a peculiarly shaped weapon from the inhabitants of the Bronze Isles.

Entire sagas have been woven about the martial valor and tireless spirit of the legendary konung Haakon the Graybeard. Under his command, the dwarves managed, if only briefly, to conquer several Skadian cities.
There aren't that many helmets or cuirasses which can survive a strike from a three-pronged hammerhead.
Some Aldorian healers place special enchantments on their staves, much to the displeasure of village priests.
A solid choice when fighting on foot.
A solid choice when fighting on foot. This particular shield bears King Etbert's crest.
A solid choice when fighting on foot. This particular shield bears an insignia of some noble house.
A frequent joke among the free companies is to hand one of these to a fresh recruit during drill, then stand back and enjoy the spectacle.
Even though a strike from this club is unlikely to harm a well-armored opponent, it still has all the chances to briefly knock the wind out of them.
The ornate pommel bears the crest of yet another noble house that did not survive the war.

The enigmatic Sages, who made the deepest thicket of the local forests their home, are one of the greatest Aldorian mysteries. Many believe it was them who'd been protecting the land from the worst of it - and now that their numbers have dwindled, everything comes crashing down.
Even though it looks plain and unassuming, putting this wooden ring on your finger brings in the surge of incredible vigor.

The reclusive Sages are long-time protectors of Aldor, yet very little is known about them. There seems to be a growing rumor that many of them have disappeared without a trace over the past few years.
Inexplicably, the strips of bark still appear to be fresh, alive even... What power makes it possible?
A tree branch, darkened from age and covered with strange etchings - simply looking at it fills you with a sense of deep-seated anxiety.
The forces from beyond the veil are fickle and vindictive. It's frighteningly easy to fall victim to them if you don't exercise the necessary caution.
A pair of sturdy boots made out of cow leather. In times of war, this sort of footwear greatly grows in demand.
These staves, still bearing the mark of the Host, are the main proof of the clerical estate's divine patronage.
To this very day, certain noble Firn lineages still preserve the tradition of instructing male heirs in the art of the sling.
Prone to superstition, Hilda believes the amulet her mother gave her long ago is the very thing that helped her survive on the island.
Hilda crafted this spear herself from the debris washed ashore after the shipwreck. Her hands remember every scratch and groove on the weathered shaft.

The Blades were a group of assassins operating in every major Aldorian city. They didn't last long though: betrayed by their client, most of them were found out, apprehended, and executed after a series of high-profile murders.
A sturdy garment that can take a beating, a reasonable choice for most contracts.
Apparently, it was forged by a commoner with a dream of heroic feats.
Engraved on the blade is a phrase in Sentian Aldorian: "To Gill Korde, for the valor and courage demonstrated by him and his troops at the Battle of Arnem."
It looks like one of the polehammers awarded by Duke Arno di Berro to captains of the Falcon Plume company for their display of heroism during the defense of the Northern Gates.
A woolen hood with a playfully folded tip. These were once popular among Maen townsmen.
A hounskull visor may look peculiar, but it actually excels at deflecting blows to the face while also providing more breathing room.
A hounskull visor may look peculiar, but it actually excels at deflecting blows to the face while also providing more breathing room.
A handy tool that can be used to carve a path through the thicket, chop some wood, or skin a dead animal.
Crossbows are well-suited for hunting - their accuracy and stopping power leave little chance even to large animals.
It will send an arrow from one end of the glade to the other, but whether it hits the squirrel in the branches - that depends on your aim.
A long-established peasant weapon for hunting small game, demanding both training and a fair bit of dexterity.
A scourge of deer, wolves, and boars, this weapon was made for large game.
Dwarven huscarls sleep in their clothes, keeping weapons and shields within arm's reach. Life in Fjall is unpredictable, and you never know when your master may require protection.
The black color of this mantle indicates that a student wearing it hasn't yet decided which Circle they wish to pursue.
Passable as a club, though each blow bends it further out of shape.
Before taking over the rest of the Jacinth Kingdoms, jade first became fashionable in Shenfen, where most of it is currently mined and produced.
A peculiar imitation of eastern blades, this sword is a product of the recent fascination with all things elven.
All these spikes offer plenty of options: can use them to intercept an incoming strike, pull down a rider from their horse, finish off a wounded opponent...
In southern regions of Ahjat, it would be considered a traditional coming-of-age gift to a young free man.
Entire wars have been fought in Fjall to claim such a sword - and the title that comes with it.
Given sufficient materials, time, and patronage, dwarven craftsmen are still capable of producing true masterpieces.
A cape made of the thinnest of silks. The material is incredibly durable: tearing even a single strip from it would require a significant effort.
Half the stories about distant Jibey one way or another mentions its legendary scimitars. Acquiring one in Aldor costs a fortune.
A gold amulet, generously painted with azure and purple. Such colors are a rare sight in Aldor.
A reminder of strenuous, yet rewarding days spent studying at the Academy, when the future looked bright and full of promise. So much has changed since then...
A uniform of the Academy student, fashioned by Jonna from a man's mantle.
Used to be generously festooned with scalps and severed fingers, making things rather awkward with the Aldorian folk, forcing Jorgrim to hastily get rid of it all.
The craftsmanship is undoubtedly dwarven, but the imagery has little to do with Fjall... Makes you wonder how exactly this pendant came into Jorgrim's possession.
Some of its combat effectiveness was stripped away in favor of imposing aesthetics. Still, it's hard to deny that this armor makes quite an impression.
The flowers adorning its brow symbolize nobility, temperance, and purity of intention.
The flowers adorning its brow symbolize nobility, temperance, and purity of intention.
It's embroidered with heraldic crests of a long dead noble house.

The main purpose of jousts is to show off, so no expense is too great when it comes to elaborate shield paintings. In fact, the last joust before the war was so exceedingly festive that it was more reminiscent of a carnival than a celebration of combat prowess.
If you ignore the lavish decorations, it's just a simple, hopelessly outdated topfhelm: the only thing it'll help you conquer it is the hearts of fair maidens.
To make a kalkan, nomads weave reed stalks into a desired shape, then fasten it with a metal ring. The resulting shield is both lightweight and durable.
A sword of newfangled design that has been growing in popularity among the mercenaries of the Grand Magistrate.
A kettle helmet is essentially a hat, albeit made of metal. Even an amateurish village blacksmith can be tasked with crafting one.
Too dull to cut anything but cheese.
Although this shield offers reliable cover to the entire body, from feet to shoulders, you won't run far with it weighing you down.
This bascinet's visor forms a solid barrier against blades and arrows. As a trade-off, it also greatly limits your vision.
This bascinet's visor forms a solid barrier against blades and arrows. As a trade-off, it also greatly limits your vision.
Making a single suit of noble plate armor demands multiple craftsmen and months of hard work.
Such axes are well-suited for mounted combat. Aldorian knights often choose them over the rest of their arsenal when they ride into battle.
The art of engraving has reached amazing heights over the last half century: each of the belt's plates is inscribed with scenes of hunts and battles.
A single glance at this noble blade is all it takes to realize that only a select few are worthy to wield it.
In war, anything can go wrong: the mail around the neck and jaw serves to keep the head where it belongs, even after losing the helmet.
Flails are considered to be a weapon of the low-born, but sometimes they are used by lords as well. Such "noble" flails are crafted with quality in mind.
Knights are demanding customers, expecting both excellent combat performance and elegant appearance from their weapon - hence the elaborate shaping of the head and handle.
The satin-lined cuffs are merely another way to parade one's impeccable taste in armor.
Invented by an unknown Aldwynn smith, this design quickly caught the eye of the local knights.
A chestpiece held in high esteem by Aldorian knights. It provides almost the same level of protection as full plate but costs less and looks fancier.
The shift in attitude towards this decidedly non-chivalrous weapon was driven by the rising number of treatises extolling the benefits and virtues of sparring with flails.
Perfectly suited for knocking the life out of your opponent with a single well-placed strike, once you've gotten them off-balance.
Aldorian knights are slow to accept novelty, but the numerous advantages of poleaxes can persuade even the most stubborn.
Polehammers have recently begun to rise in popularity as a weapon of choice for knightly jousts.
Well-suited for fighters who want their every strike to leave the opponent in the dirt.
Sometimes a knight needs to fight on foot. This shield is excellent for such scenarios.
This two-handed sword was created with pure functionality in mind: its strength lies in perfect simplicity and great balance.
This weapon is a world apart from the tool which originally inspired it - a humble pickaxe.
In the Aldorian tradition, a sword is the only "knightly" weapon there is. Lately, however, the nobility has been demonstrating a growing appreciation for warhammers and maces.
Can break one's jaw just by trying to spell its name.

It is said that in the very heart of the desert between Hazzun and Al-Qaza lies the Labyrinth, guarded by silent warriors. Whatever is kept within remains secret, for no one who entered it has ever returned alive and sane.
A quilted chestpiece reinforced with multiple rows of leather plates to better disperse the force of incoming strikes.
A weapon tested by millennia of hunting and gathering.
There's something ostensibly pagan about it... Who was it that collected all this lazurite, which is almost non-existent in Aldor, and by what means, is anyone's guess.
A perfectly ordinary leather belt, nothing more.
Decent protection against scrapes and bites - better than nothing for the hunt or a brawl.
These gloves were crafted from finely tanned leather.
The Firns' descendants strictly adhere to tradition: these shields are almost exactly the same as the ones used a thousand years ago.
Comfortable and pragmatic, such vests are favored by peasants with spare gold in their pockets.

One of the many rules of the Gymnasium forbade removing these heavy and uncomfortable bracers anywhere but the baths - Leosthenes has grown so accustomed to their presence that he now sees them as just another part of his body.
A Nistrian-style doublet, reeking of sweat and blood. The only reminder of its owner's Agemon past is a few gilded clasps bearing the crest of the Great Despot.
Some peasants own only a shirt on their back. Naturally, it's worn for every occasion.
A lucky hit from such a flail can squash a cheap helmet along with the wearer's skull.
It protects well for its price. The main concern is overheating to death.
A shield that combines the convenience of heater shields with the coverage of kite shields. On the downside, it's rather heavy.
This axe is great for felling trees. It fares only slightly worse when used for chopping heads.

Be it Aldorian troubadours, Nistrian vagantes, or elven gusans - a lute is beloved by minstrels all over the world. Recently it made its way even to Fjall, although not every skald was happy with such a frivolous turn of events. Many of them refuse to play the instrument, deeming it a major threat to their long-established craft and musical tradition.
The buckle is encrusted with a real ruby, greatly increasing its appeal in the eyes of any mage.
This helmet's original owner was Lord Nothward, notorious for his obsession with weapons and armor. Rather peculiarly, he was also an irredeemable coward insistent on avoiding the battlefield.
This helmet's original owner was Lord Nothward, notorious for his obsession with weapons and armor. Rather peculiarly, he was also an irredeemable coward insistent on avoiding the battlefield.
Maafs are the warrior estate which seized power in Ahjat a few centuries ago. Their round shields are hard to mistake for anything else.
Soldiers of the Maen garrison hold a deep appreciation for simplicity, light weight, and convenience. This crossbow combines all three qualities in its design.
The majority of the Cowls, the fanatical followers of Neer, are former peasants, so it comes as no surprise that their favorite weapon is a heavy flail.
Most of the time, the weather in Maen is rather chilly, so local mages have a preference for thick, heavy mantles. Why risk catching a cold if you can avoid it?
For years, the Maen Academy was the crucible that forged battlemages in service of the crown. Predictably, it fell on hard times after the King's death and the rise of the Gray Army.

Kasper Stern, a legendary sorcerer and mentor at the Maen Academy, lived about a century ago and was an exceptionally versatile individual. Conrad II Ironhand held the mage in such high esteem that he entrusted him with leading a part of the royal army - an unprecedented decision!
The leather was specially treated to protect against fire, electricity, and other harmful elements that may be summoned by a resisting sorcerer.
The life of a magehunter is full of uncertainty. However, with a gambeson like this, managing that uncertainty becomes so much easier.
A regular quilted gambeson, painted with the blues and whites of the Magistrate. It's often worn by village militias and light infantry.
A solid choice when fighting on foot. This particular shield bears the colors of the Grand Magistrate.
A mail shirt with a blue and white tabard - a standard chestpiece of the Magistrate's garrisons.
The steps taken in these felt boots surpass even the number of stars in the sky.

A manner of robe that is typically the sole possession of wandering dervishes such as Mahir. It offers reliable protection from both the scorching sun of Jacinth as well as the bone-chilling cold of its desert nights.
Such footwear is cheap to maintain and can withstand a glancing blow to the legs - what else can a warrior wish for?
Additional neck protection is never redundant. Still, it pays not to get overly confident while wearing something as light as a cervellier.
Mail coifs are ubiquitous - they don't cost much, are lightweight, and provide adequate protection against slashing strikes.
Sensible handwear for anyone who values their fingers.
Such mantles have been a common garb of Aldorian battlemages ever since the Radiant Wars - the long range of maaf bows would make short work of anyone wearing light clothes.
It seems like someone tried to improvise a battle axe of sorts. Not the worst result, all things considered.
A shepherd's ol' reliable, perfectly adequate for driving stray wolves away from the flock.
An indispensable tool for pinning down fugitives and wild beasts, it can often be seen in the hands of bounty hunters.
The outer rim is decorated with an elegant pattern - a technique typical of Maen jewelers.
Somewhat reminiscent of the bands exchanged between newly-weds in Maen - or at least that had been the custom when people there were still getting married.
Over the last few decades, Aldorians have attained incredible mastery in forging heavy plate armor. Axes like this are simply an attempt at evening the odds.
A thin belt complete with a few metal buckles. It's still more of a fashion accessory than a functional piece of armor though.
A crossbow fitted with a more sophisticated cranequin than usual, allowing for a noticeably smoother reloading process.
A breastplate worn by lonely mercenaries - it's relatively light, covers the vital spots, and doesn't cost all that much.
Light, durable, sits well in hand, and can most certainly put an arrogant knight in his place.
Halberds were originally a mercenary weapon, so their widespread use among the Grand Magistrate's soldiers is hardly surprising - at least half of them hail from free companies.
The life of a mercenary is too short and violent for dull outfits.
The longer mercenaries stay alive, the better they're paid - and the better they're paid, the longer they stay alive...
As mercenaries like to jest, the longer the sword, the shorter the opposition.
Among the Magistrate mercenaries, polehammers are almost as widespread as halberds.
Not all free companies are sent to the front - some are contracted to protect supply convoys and undertake other auxiliary duties.
About a third of someone' contract reward went toward the paints and hiring an artist. Well, we only live once.
A heavy warhammer is one of the few weapons that can give an infantryman a fighting chance against a dismounted knight.
Every self-respecting merchant has a similar dagger. It can add more weight to your words when dealing with disgruntled clients - unless they come in numbers.
A well-to-do city merchant can be easily recognized by their attire. Such flashiness worked a bit too well in Maen - anyone dressed in a fancy garb was among the first to face the gallows.
Every now and then, the clergy and nobility consider outlawing this weapon - with a billhook in hand even a greenhorn can stand his ground against the heavy cavalry.
Peasants call these clubs "snails" - the curled head adds weight to every blow.
Will slice through a brigand's gambeson or a beast's hide - and for the militia, it's all that's needed.
Village militias are called upon only as a last resort. And when the going gets dire, even a farming tool can become a weapon.
Gather a group of peasants, provide them with a handful of longaxes and a clever sergeant, and even a better-equipped opponent might find itself stumped on how to deal with the resulting force.
Add a bunch of cheaply-made spikes, and suddenly a simple mace becomes far more deadly and menacing.
A home-made axe on a long pole - this weapon sees frequent use in the hands of the Gray Army.
Looks like it used to be a common hammer until some smith reinforced it with additional metal and put it on a longer shaft.
When defending one's home, no weapon is too humble.
It doesn't take much time to learn how to use a spear efficiently. For this very reason, it became almost synonymous with peasant militias.
Peasants are seldom accustomed to close combat, so outfitting them with slings is a sound strategy - though usually overlooked by noble lords.
The idea of strapping a round metal chestplate to a gambeson has its roots in Nistra, but the benefits it provides quickly became apparent to Aldorian armorsmiths as well.
Knightly etiquette dictates that a mercy blow to an incapacitated opponent can only be delivered with this dagger.
Aldorian monks' staves are blessed by the Host themself to help the faithful endure the hardships of daily toil and distant travels.
A hit or two, and this morgenstern will be the last star your enemy will ever see.
Gold, silver, morion... Looks like someone wanted to have a piece of jewelry for every occasion.
Staring for too long into the mirror-polished buckle summons a creeping sensation of falling into the abyss.
They're covered with peculiar symbols in accordance with an ancient, half-forgotten technique.
The trade fleet of Brynn is small but well equipped - each sailor is required to always carry a hatchet in case of a pirate attack.

Necromancers aren't bereft of a sense of humor, albeit theirs comes with a morbid twist: most of them find joy in desecrating graves of the clergy, using harvested skulls as a decoration for their staves.
It didn't take long for Fjall dwarves to adopt Skadian bardiches. And thanks to their mercenaring varangs, this weapon shape became known to Nistrians as well.
The Zeineshes of Nistra are among the most skilled mounted archers on the entire Continent, rivaled only by the nomads of the Ohirian barrens and Jarimad.
A typical weapon of Zeinesh mountain brigands. During times of conflict, they descend to the flatlands, offering to fight for the highest bidder.

Nistra is a fascinating land of many intertwined cultures. Nistrian nobles, who still stay true to the ancient traditions of Axonia, also eagerly embrace the customs of Pjesht highlanders - such as a preference for this style of cloak, for example.
Constant wars with Aldor left a deep mark on Nistrian warfare. Naturally, they've become quite successful at combating heavily armored troops.
Even to this day, Nistrian smiths continue producing certain types of antique weaponry, although they use steel instead of iron now.

To the Great Despot it is a matter of prestige to be perceived as a successor to the great Axonian Empire and its rulers. This is why so many Nistrian armor and weaponsmiths continue to dutifully replicate centuries-old designs.
Some nobles wouldn't mind bartering away one of their smaller, less prosperous fiefs in exchange for a similar chestpiece.
No armor can resist a blow dealt with such a monstrously heavy axeblade.
A fashionable Aldwynn style, marked by unmistakable Nistrian motifs, likely adopted from passing merchants.
With the exception of Aldwynn weaponsmiths, there aren't that many craftsmen in Aldor who can produce a mace of similar quality.
As the centuries-old tradition of knightly ransom becomes a thing of the past, aristocratic weapons grow increasingly more deadly, aiming to kill the opponent right on the spot.
Two-handed flails may have originated on tournament grounds, but it's a rare occurrence to see a noble lord using one as he goes to war.
It could have easily belonged to either a small-time baron or a duke - basically the kind of person who doesn't skimp on expanding their arsenal.
The red velvet fittings are here to remind everyone who the lord of the land is.
Horsemen of the Ohirian barrens usually prefer adargas over kalkans: a light leather shield is much more convenient when fighting from horseback.
Tying coins to a belt is an Ohirian custom, each of them commemorating an important event in the wearer's life: surviving a battle, becoming a parent, losing a loved one...
The edge requires surprisingly little maintenance. If the rumors are true, the tribes of the Yellow Steppe achieve this by treating their swords with some special solution.
A weapon frequently carried by the nomads of the Ohirian barrens - every tribesman is given one after surviving his first battle.
For many, the first clash with Jarimad mounted archers is also the last: deadly on foot, they are even deadlier from the saddle.
The Ohirian barrens offer nothing but sand and rocks, yet the tribes who dwell there make do with little else.
The dwarves of the Norrheim clan have to deal with the most dangerous beasts the North can offer on a daily basis, so their warriors' garments are frequently imbued with protective enchantments.

If a piece of armor is decorated with wild animals' horns, furs, and bones, then it most likely belonged to a member of the Norrheim clan, where a person's merit is based exclusively on their hunting prowess.
A traditional weapon of Fjall clans, first adopted by the guards protecting the Northern Borderlands and then - by the rest of the kingdom.
In the North, there still persists a barbaric tradition of decorating belts with various so-called "trophies".
Skirmishing, plundering, murdering - nothing is impossible with this blade!
They are waterproof, don't get stuck in mud, and are easy to clean from blood stains.
Dwarves don't hold much respect for bows. They are usually handed out to youngsters who aren't experienced enough to fight on the frontline.
The dwarves of Fjall rarely use spears in combat, deeming them a hunting weapon, unworthy of the battlefield.
The path to becoming a mage invariably starts with pain and injuries. A few layers of tightly wrapped cloth can slightly reduce the price of initial failures.
Not particularly durable, but it serves its purpose well enough.

Long ago, Brynn was a gathering spot for a secret cult of sanguimancers, coveting the secrets of vampirism. It's unknown whether they were successful with their experiments, but they surely met their demise as it befits vampires - with wooden stakes deep in their chests.
A relatively affordable piece of plate armor, worn by landless knights, mercenaries, and everyone else who isn't rolling in gold at the moment.
Battered, antiquated, and possibly passed down from someone's great-grandfather.
A ragged shirt littered with stitches, patches, and oily spots.
Thanks to its domed shape, a bascinet can reliably redirect incoming strikes, preventing them from doing significant harm.
Some people spitefully suggest that, contrary to their name, sentinets weren't invented in Sentia but were brought there from the Bronze Isles. The truth is, no one knows for sure anymore.
Thanks to these pridefully displayed amulets, one can easily spot an oracle on the streets of Hazzun.
Back in the day, these flails were a symbol of the exceptional strength and impeccable training demonstrated by the marshals of the Triune Hand Order.
The gilded acanthus ornaments are a staple of Hazzun jewelry. This technique was invented in ancient times and has seen very little change after being rediscovered by modern craftsmen.
Exquisite carvings, smooth curves... Everything about this crossbow betrays its elven origins.
Jarimad nomads fight on foot only when there's no other option, but when they do, they rely on these axes - the design proved its usefulness through many battles against the heavy maaf infantry.
Elven shields are reliable and don't weigh the arm down. The alloy they are made of is exotic to Aldor.
At the end of the day, few things can beat fine elven craftsmanship: it clearly took a lot of skill to create a staff this magnificent.
Forged in the fabled land of Shanguo, this shield had traveled for thousands of miles before finding its way to Aldor - it's nigh impossible to imagine the circumstances of its journey.
A generously gilded helmet you'd be expected to wear while leading parades in celebration of ceaseless victories.
A prize from the Aldwynn Tournament of 407, claimed by Sir Tibalt der Wald, who impressed the King himself by hitting five moving targets squarely in the center in just one minute.
Local noblemen have an ancient tradition of exchanging daggers. As a rule, it's done by plunging them into each other's back.
It's clear as day that the master smith, who meticulously crafted this weapon, put his heart and soul into it. The lord ordering it must have had money to spare.
At some point, it likely belonged to a Sentian patrician with a keen interest in the weapon traditions of the Bronze Isles.
Pairs well with any mantle, making it perfect for mages who care about fashion.
The blade is inscribed with words of dedication to one Idrich Jorg of Aldwynn.
A customary weapon of the Royal Armorbreakers - a renowned guard unit currently employed in the service of the Council.
The gilded guard is decorated with intricate eastern ornaments - this weapon is a true work of art.
A sallet painted in black with a line of prayer meticulously inscribed on its rim - supposedly, it should provide additional protection against stray arrows and sudden strikes.
To the nobility, the sling is more a plaything than a true weapon, fit only for occasional displays of skill at a tournament.
The stamp of the Guild makes it safe to assume that this voulge was intended for some high-ranking Magistrate commander.
The sentiment that maces are a weapon of the peasantry is long obsolete. Adorned with gold and horsehair, this warhammer demonstrates it better than anything.
When properly sharpened, the broad edges of the "ox tongue" spearhead bite into flesh, leaving gruesome, slow-to-heal wounds.
It'll make you sweat like a pig, but safety is above all.
It's suitable for a night out, a duel, or both at the same time. You'll look appropriate either way.
An attire that would be the main source of pride for any show-off from the slums.
This sinister amulet is older than a century or two.
On the Bronze Isles, every dispute is resolved in a duel. These daggers are a product of the times.
The protrusions on both sides of the spearhead allow for some fancy moves, such as parrying sluggish sword strikes.
This necklace is not made of the best pearls, but it wouldn't be fair to call it cheap-looking.
These bows can often be found in possession of some particularly bold peasants, who secretly hunt on their lord's land.
The only distinguishing feature about this cap is that it hasn't been washed for way too long.
During peacetime these flails are used to thresh wheat. During wartime - to chase off marauding deserters.
In case of heat or rain, its brims can be slightly turned down.
The only thing it's decent at is splitting firewood.
An old farm tool, hastily repurposed for war - neither sturdy nor comfortable to wield.
Makes short work of haystacks and distracted opponents.
A sash with the nastiest timing for getting loose, so it's best to fasten it as tight as possible.
When a peasant war is about to erupt, a scythe is as good a weapon as any.
Such shoes are usually worn until they crumble right off the wearer's feet.
A plain homemade spear, handy for driving off wolves that follow your cart a little too closely.
If the old tales are to be trusted, Saint Dekar once struck down a forest troll with a sling much like this.
A vessel for someone's soul, sealed by a forbidden ritual. It always pays to stay on your toes when dealing with dark magic...
These pickaxes see use all over the realm, from the gold mines of Forholt to the convict quarries of the Northern Borderlands.
Bascinets with such visors are novel to Aldor, but they are growing more and more fashionable with each passing day.
Bascinets with such visors are novel to Aldor, but they are growing more and more fashionable with each passing day.
A trusty companion for long journeys between holy sites.
The tip of this unsettling mask's beak is packed with a mix of medicinal herbs, their smell repelling plague-bearing miasmas. That's how most healers explain it anyway.
Crafting plate gloves is a form of art which demands consummate skill and impeccable knowledge of the trade.
Wrist mobility is of little importance when it takes a small squad of soldiers to get through your armor.
A design that has been the pinnacle of peasant fashion for the last twenty years.
Its glory years are long behind it. In this day and age, Aldorian armorsmiths are capable of forging much more efficient designs.
The fashion for wearing gambesons as civil clothing was spearheaded by King Etbert himself. Thanks to his efforts, it became a sign of good taste to wear a pourpoint when attending the court.
The open palm on top has a strange tendency to attract glances... A truly mesmerizing feeling.
Reloading a crossbow with a lever offers much greater convenience without sacrificing the weapon's draw weight.
Fire magic can be hazardous even to pyromancers themselves. This cowl is capable of extinguishing the flames that creep dangerously close to its wearer's face.
It bears a special enchantment: the fabric is not exactly flameproof, but it's definitely slow to catch fire.
The spherical headpiece radiates extreme heat, which, paradoxically, doesn't seem to cause any harm - at least for the time being...
Though mainly a practice weapon, it can still cave in the skull of a careless foe.

In the aftermath of the Council's crushing defeat under the walls of Brynn, many patricians decided to purchase themself a quillon to at least partly resemble Duke di Berro, whose daring deeds allowed the Magistrate to emerge victorious.
The Radiant Wars offered plenty of opportunities to make a show of one's piety: the nobility spared no expense in adorning their weapons and armor with symbols of the Host.

In the times of the Radiant Wars, many high-born lords were more than happy to join King Etbert's army. Some did it to earn the Host's favor, others - for more practical reasons, such as glory and new riches.
A variant of a topfhelm improved upon by Aldorian craftsmen, boasting a slicker shape and exquisite ornaments. These were all the rage during the Radiant Wars.
It sits snugly in the grip, ideal for lightning-fast assaults on straggling enemy caravans.
A helmet most favored by dwarven fighters: inexpensive to make, lightweight, and sufficiently intimidating.
Light to carry, yet far from harmless - a weapon well suited for clashes with village guards.

The fearsome Raven Gang, which used to be the scourge of the entire realm, suffered their first and final defeat in the battle of the Hangman's Hill. There were almost no survivors: Sir Wolfard's troops immediately hanged those few bandits they had captured, giving the previously nameless hill its moniker.
As the common folk like to say, a flail is the final argument of peasants.
This wide blade is best wielded with both hands.
It would be impossible to count how many noble lords had their lives cut short by this outrageously unassuming peasant weapon.
Forging a similar sword is a graduation exam of sorts for apprentice blacksmiths.
Maimed by war, common soldiers become a heavy burden for their families, so each of them tries their best to return home with a full set of limbs.
Recruits typically need time to adjust to carrying and properly using a shield, which is why they prefer to start out with something lighter.
Despite being more dangerous than a simple makeshift club, it's still no real mace.
A crudely reforged hunting knife suitable for butchering game and slitting throats.
A flail like this can be quite unpredictable, demanding much more finesse than you might initially assume.
Upon closer inspection, it becomes apparent that this poleaxe was inspired by Skadian bardiches.
Mail shirts have stood the test of time: they were already in use when the Ald tribes, the ancestors of the modern Aldorians, took over the Firn foothills.
A single solid strike from this polemace will make your enemy forget even their own name.
Red silk is brought to Brynn by elven caravans, from where it eventually finds its way to Maen craftsmen - only they know how to work this fascinating material to unlock its full potential.
Clubs like this one are often employed by city guards - don't need anything more substantial to intimidate a pickpocket.
A mace that offers the best value for its price - it's simple, easy to wield, and utterly lethal.
This kalkan is larger and heavier than usual. Perhaps it once belonged to some nomadic nobleman.
A pair of cheek plates instantly transforms this light helmet into a noticeably more protective piece.
With enough dexterity, this staff's heavy knob is all you need to beat the living daylights out of a slow-witted brigand.
A few rows of spiked iron bands is certainly one way to add to your weapon's heftiness.
This marvelous artifact appears to be at least millennia old. It's a mystery how it hasn't yet rotted away.
A cavalryman's shield of choice - it helps with deflecting spears while on horseback and increases your chances of survival when the situation requires you to dismount.
Comfortable for long horse rides. Not so comfortable for long walks.
A direct strike to the hands will still hurt like hell, but at least you won't lose them.
Under the rule of King Etbert, the Road Guard's upkeep was often an afterthought - the soldiers had to make do with worn-down chainmail and battered brigandines.
Rondaches were brought to Aldor from the Bronze Isles. As with all things new and alien, the locals regard them with a certain amount of prejudice.
With its disk-shaped guard and pommel, this dagger offers a firm grip while keeping the blade from driving too deep into the enemy's body.
For when you've run out of other options.
A wooden shield reinforced with a few strips of metal. A common armament of the Aldorian infantry.
A sword from King Etbert's personal armory. Clearly not his favorite, otherwise it would have followed the monarch into his tomb.

Whether due to age, infirmity, or fear, some of the realm's kings steered clear of the tournament grounds, leaving the task of upholding their honor to carefully chosen and trained knights - the royal champions

A royal huntmaster is defined by three duties: the king should have plenty of game to pursue, he must never return from the hunt empty-handed, and he shall always remain safe and sound while doing so.
The right to hunt in His Majesty's preserves was among the chief privileges of the Royal Rangers. Some even adorned their hats with writs bearing the highest permission.
The main task of the Royal Rangers was to patrol the hunting grounds of His Majesty. Their gambesons were sewn by the best craftsmen the realm had to offer.
The ability of rubies to withstand violent energies makes them particularly attractive to sorcerers who favor dangerous and unpredictable schools of magic, such as pyromancy.
Mage bracers are usually made of silver or gold and are encrusted with all kinds of gems - the resonance created by putting precious metals and stones together is capable of empowering magic spells.
The ruby shines the prettiest under the direct sunlight.
A distinguishing accessory of a noble or wealthy elf.
People of Fjall hold a belief that a properly crafted runic charm can bring success in any endeavor.
Shackles so rusty that you shouldn't have any trouble removing them with your bare hands.
A sword that has seen better days. It looks like it's about to crumble to dust.
There are two kinds of halberdiers: those who favor thrusting and those who favor slashing. But in case you want to do both, this is a weapon for you.
Sallets aren't uncommon among Aldorian soldiers. An open version is comfortable to wear, compromising neither your vision nor the ability to take deep breaths.

The deserts of Ahjat are harsh, fickle, and teeming with dangers. Fortunately, the Sand Guard, tasked with escorting caravans, knows exactly how to handle them - whether it's raiding nomadic bands or something even worse...
Mage bracers are usually made of silver or gold and are encrusted with all kinds of gems - the resonance created by putting precious metals and stones together is capable of empowering magic spells.
Whenever there's a source of magic close by, this pendant begins to give off a faint, thrumming sound.

Maaf military leaders - sardars - enjoy great power even when not actively leading the armies. In the years between wars, most of them hoard so much wealth that they become almost reluctant to return to the battlefield...
Scaled armor was at its peak during the latest war with Nistra, from where it was borrowed by Aldorian knights to begin with.
These were obviously inspired by the Nistrian military tradition - scaled armor is still a common sight around those parts.
This halberd's name comes from its vague resemblance to a scorpion's tail and the ability to deliver equally painful "stings".
Brought out when a scouting mission calls for a probing strike.
The Sentians have always been known for their ability to add a touch of refinement to even the most simple and mundane items.
"As vexing as a Sentian slinger" - a saying dating back to the wars of Aldor's unification, still in use in the kingdom's western reaches.
It's a common practice for such polearms to be adorned with vibrant scraps of cloth, helping soldiers distinguish friend from foe as they head into the fray.
This small, easy to carry hatchet comes into play when a sword just doesn't cut it anymore.
Sergeants and veteran mercenaries sport this type of armor while they're saving up for something more substantial.
A well-made sword, forged from good steel and completely bereft of any trifling decorations.
Poleaxes started to gain popularity in Aldor not long before the war, as the need to combat the increasing use of heavy armor had been becoming more and more apparent.
Not the most light or comfortable footwear, but, as the saying goes, victory trumps everything.
A weapon often used by sergeants of the Magistrate. Such blades aren't cheap but definitely worth it.
Countless Nistrian commanders, standard-bearers, and mages were left forever in Aldorian soil during the Nistrian Wars. The archers responsible could expect little mercy in return.

It's a well-known fact that the Brotherhood of Truth's Eternal Sun favors the pursuit of Knowledge over prayers and martial training. And yet, it doesn't affect the Brotherhood knights' combat readiness in the slightest: if anything, their study of the world's secrets fills them with incredible zeal on the battlefield.
A shishpar is a flanged mace used by soldiers of Jibey and the Maaf Empire. It particularly excels at punching through scaled armor, which is quite popular in those lands.
A makeshift blade wrapped in a piece of cloth. A common weapon of urchins and vagabonds.
Sickles are generally wielded by women and children - during the harvesting season, they are tasked with cleaning up patches of grain that were missed during scything.
The brutal killing power of this crossbow gave birth to rumors that even soldiers hiding behind mantlets aren't safe from the bolts launched with it.

A squad of competent engineers can determine the outcome of an entire siege. Therefore, it pays to keep them well-protected and properly armed: tunnel skirmishes beneath the walls are a common occurrence.
Amethyst is also known as the savant's stone - pendants and necklaces encrusted with this gem are often worn by people who have a keen interest in various sciences.
A ring encrusted with an amethyst is a well-known talisman for maintaining mental clarity.
When purchasing jewelry, mages prioritize craftsmanship and a correct balance between gems and metals, both of which are vital if one hopes to utilize the materials' mystical properties.
It's believed that every aquamarine carries a heavenly blessing, so they are often used for rings and necklaces.
The Emir of Jibey buys the loyalty of Ohirian warlords with gold, silk, and amulets not unlike this one - doing so is much cheaper than sending in the army to reestablish his rule over the region.
In iatrochemistry, emeralds are thought to be the quintessence of life force - which is why they are worn day and night by those obsessed with longevity.
Jewelry connoisseurs might wrinkle their noses at mixing gold and silver, but hardly anyone else shares the objection.
Jade isn't as popular in Aldor as it is in the East, but the jewelry made with it is still pretty easy to find.
According to local beliefs, silver offers protection against the evil eye and diseases.
A frozen spark of the setting sun, now on your finger.
Jewelers consider the union of silver and sapphires to be one of the most refined and harmonious.
Topaz and silver form an exquisite combination, priced precisely to appeal to the wives of respectable merchants.
Skadian bardiches are a weapon of status. Only the greatest warriors - the champions and the retinue of Skadian princes - are allowed to carry them.
A bludgeon is a surprisingly deadly piece of gear. If anyone knows their maces, it's Skadians.
These axes don't see much use in Aldor, but in Skadia they are ubiquitous. If the axeblade ever gets too chipped to use, you can always switch your grip and strike with the hammerhead.
Generations of conflicts with Fjall dwarves and Jarimad nomads left an indelible mark of their culture on Skadian armor and weaponry.
A shield bearing the crest of the Elder Prince, likely brought to Aldor by a mercenary who once served in the Prince's retinue.

This weapon can often be seen in the hands of Skadian horsemen. They weren't the ones who first started using it though: Skadians borrowed the design from the Poleds, a tribe inhabiting the shores of the Icy Sea.
Distant Skadia is notable for its powerful spellcasters, known as volkhvs. There must be quite a story to this staff.
Skadians borrowed the practice of reinforcing mail armor with iron plates from Jarimad warriors. Such chestpieces are more than capable of protecting the cavalry from stray arrows and spear thrusts.
There's a dwarven belief that an attire blessed by Skarhogg shamans can fortify strength and resolve of any warrior who puts it on.
Skirmishers are expected to carry a considerable amount of arrows and gear, so they give more thought than most to their choice of belts and bandoleers.
A favorite shield of the Nistrian light cavalry - its curved form protects a good portion of the rider's back from arrows.
Whittling the enemy down with agile maneuvering and constant arrow barrages from a safe distance is the preferred tactic of the Nistrian light infantry.
Attrition through small engagements is certainly one way to tip the decisive battle in your favor.
Skonfert is famous for its furnaces and masterful smiths. In times of great conflict, it was there that victories were forged.

"Sky-readers" was the name given by King Etbert's soldiers to the elven oracles who fought on the Jacinth's side during the Radiant Wars. Many attribute the Aldorian army's defeat to their involvement, for how can one defeat an enemy who sees the outcome of every battle in advance?
The sooner you start, the better your chances of mastering the bow - which is why in Aldorian villages training begins in early childhood.
A crossbow used by both sides of the conflict - it's equally popular with the soldiers of the Council and the mercenaries of the Magistrate.
Rapid advancements in steel forging are slowly but steadily driving down the cost of plate armor - enough that wealthier foot soldiers can now afford individual pieces.
You never know, it just might save your life one day.
