Some taverns insist such gloves are to be left at the door - the generous metal plating is deemed reason enough.
It takes a truly impressive amount of strength and stamina to handle this axe efficiently.
As the boastful proverb goes, as long as Aldor has at least a dozen longbows, it'll remain unconquered.
Even plate armor can only do so much when it catches a solid hit from a full-cast flailhead.

One of King Etbert's decrees demanded all mages to join the army in the event of war, which handily provided him with lots of additional troops during his Radiant campaigns. These army-issued mantles aren't much to look at, but the craftsmanship is perfectly serviceable.
Such poleaxes are forged by the Council weaponsmiths with less metal than usual, allowing for cheaper production.
If blunt force alone isn't enough to get through your opponent's armor, this polemace also has a spike for precise jabs to the neck.
The siege caught Brynn unprepared, and the defenders soon realized their supply of arrows would not last. To combat this, they quickly crafted and distributed a great number of slings.
The magic sigils carved into the leather are useful for swiftly redirecting the flow of magical energies in the chaos of combat.
After surviving the Academy and the first handful of battles, most mages usually seek to invest their earnings into a proper staff.

The Faceless are the Palace Guard of the Great Jacinth and are considered the best warriors in all the Elven Kingdoms. Among other feats, they distinguished themselves on the frontlines of the Radiant Wars by repelling the advance of King Etbert's army.
Spellweavers perceive the world very differently from other people, their minds finely tuned to sense magical disturbances in their surroundings.
Intended to be worn with pride as a visible badge of a Spellweaver's high standing and exceptional mastery of magic.

Most mages have to contend with merely reciting the few surviving spells, possessing little to no understanding of the force powering them. But not everyone is satisfied with this state of things: the half-secretive Circle of Spellweavers aims to resurrect the lost art of creating new spells - their efforts are yet to bear fruit.
The ranks of Spellweavers only accept the best of the best - even prominent Academy mentors often find themself snubbed and rebuked.
Unlike a common pitchfork, a spetum has its prongs pointed at different directions, which makes them much more suitable for catching enemy blades.
The spikes alone are menacing enough to make any tavern loudmouth reconsider his words.
It's best not to find yourself on the receiving end of this warclub - its strikes not only shatter bones but also leave deep puncture wounds.
A cheap yet effective protection from sudden strikes to the feet.
Readily provided to levies by most lords: once the war is over, commoners are expected to return to their crafts and fieldwork, and for that they still need their hands.
Another great example of the Bronze Isles craftsmanship, where a weapon's aesthetics is prized just as much as its killing potential.
With this coif on your head, it's only appropriate to join a quest for some holy chalices or rocks with a sword stuck in them.
It's customary for a squire to have his shield painted in his master's colors to limit the confusion that may arise in the midst of a raging battle.
A natural pick for a marksman - or any soldier who values dexterity and precision.
King Etbert used to personally award such rings to soldiers who distinguished themselves during his military campaigns.
Slender and elegant - like a sonnet spoken over the grave of an old foe.
Tanned bull hide guarantees longevity for any footwear it's used in.
It's completely soaked in blood and sweat.
There is an etching on the blade's surface, pledging loyalty to the Host and the court... the usual kind of court.
Templars of the Order united chivalric discipline and magic mastery with the relentless study of the Host - all in the hopes of earning the gift of thaumaturgy.
The legendary templar Theobrand was born and raised in a poor shepherd's family. A master of the sling since childhood, he kept up the practice even after joining the Order.
Forged by great weaponsmiths in times immemorial, this blade still carries the blessing of the Host.
A risky weapon for those who are confident in their skill.
The trick is not to tighten the bindings too much. Numb hands are of little use in a fight.
Topazes can be relatively cheap - pieces of equipment encrusted with them are commonly purchased by mages of modest income.
Topfhelms have been out of fashion for quite a while. Nowadays they are worn almost exclusively by gray-haired, irredeemably nostalgic veterans.
The growing prevalence of archers and crossbowmen keeps driving shields to become taller, thicker, and heavier.
Fancy enough for when you're going out into the world to prove your mettle.
It's significantly easier to learn how to use a crossbow than a bow, but there's no avoiding practice either way.
A simple wooden bow that is remarkably tricky to land a shot with.
It'll keep the rain away, help you stay warm in cold weather, and conceal you in the dark.
Shoes like these are cheap and wear well, the qualities which attract all kinds of vagabonds. Just make sure to keep them free of holes.
Any journey feels easier with a staff in hand.
Both the soldiers of the Northern Borderlands and the dwarves they are getting in constant skirmishes with have a morbid penchant for collecting fingers of slain foes and wearing them as necklaces.
This mysterious armor looks both surprisingly modern and incredibly ancient...
The absolute mystery of this helmet's origins deepens with its engraved interior, which calls to mind elven craftsmanship - though, given the overall design, such a claim makes very little sense.
The absolute mystery of this helmet's origins deepens with its engraved interior, which calls to mind elven craftsmanship - though, given the overall design, such a claim makes very little sense.
Doesn't make you look any more trustworthy, to put it bluntly.
This staff saturates the surrounding air with a heavy stench of stale blood.
Seemingly cumbersome, this steel staff is actually quite light. It's also an excellent conduit for magic.
The backbone of the Magistrate's army, the Vanguard, is formed by mercenaries. A decent pay, plentiful drinks, and honorary swords make for a surefire way to earn the loyalty of the dogs of war.
Left behind by the Ancestors, the Vaults conceal boundless riches and unspeakable horrors alike. Only the most capable warriors have the courage to explore their forsaken halls.
The ease of maintenance and lethal power of flails make them a staple choice among the Vehement, particularly in battles against the undead.
The Vehement are militant Hieronites who took up arms to fight against all manner of ghouls, warlocks, and unholy monstrosities.
When it comes to the Vehement, their members hail from all walks of life - there are peasants, fanatical pilgrims, knights errant, and even battlemages.
The initiation into the Vehement is incomplete without a sacred vow: to stand always with the Hieron and the common folk, doing all in one's power to purge the evils of this world
Quite a few mages are drawn to the Vehement ranks, seeking to apply their knowledge and skills for the benefit of the common folk.
A well-made caftan, now battered and worn out, a distant echo of the life left behind.
This ring used to belong to Velmir's brother. Now it serves as a silent reminder of the path he chose for himself.

For a long while, there existed an unspoken rule in Aldor that only those born into nobility can wear red velvet. However, during the last few decades, Brynn merchants have been showing increasingly less respect towards this custom.
It's set with an incredibly large morion that seems to be shifting its color every now and then... Or is it merely a trick of the light?
In knowledgeable hands, this staff can turn even the weakest poison into a deadly one.
Upon parting with this ring, Verren was, as always, stingy on details. It might come in handy at some point... But how?
Built to persevere - even cleaving it apart would take more than a single strike.
Armed with such a bow, an experienced archer can single-handedly stall the advance of a full squad of light infantry.
Properly outfitting a commoner for war typically requires a collective effort, sometimes involving the entire extended family.
The presence of a dagger on one's belt is what sets apart an experienced warrior from a greenhorn: it gives you an indisputable edge the moment the fight moves to the ground.
Only a seasoned fighter can handle a two-headed flail without embarrassing themselves.
Noticeably sturdier than standard soldier gloves - proof that their owner has lasted long enough to amass some decent coin.
An obvious choice for any infantryman: a combination of common boots' practicality and some fairly decent protection.
It's hard to imagine armor which would protect from a direct hit with this axe.
The two-handed flail is hardly the most widespread weapon. The reason is simple: few soldiers possess the strength, skill, and finesse to master it.
A reliable weapon which doesn't require much skill or maintenance, an easy choice for many common soldiers.
An axe capable of cleaving open both the laminar armor of maaf riders and the breastplates of Nistrian cataphracts.
The convenience and versatility of these polehammers garner them praise from the veteran soldiers of both the Council and the Magistrate.
The mace head is shaped in such a way that it's equally effective against both lightly and heavily armored opponents.
As one prominent Aldorian commander used to say, wars are waged by knights but are won with spears. Spears like this one.
A soldier adept with the sling can be every bit as lethal as a seasoned archer or crossbowman.
This sword has witnessed many a front line and survived plenty of close battles.
Sometimes the simplest solution is the best one - this maul is a testament to it.

In the chaotic months following the uprising, the Magistrate struggled to contain the surge of crime that swept over Brynn. The tide turned with the rise of vigilantes - well-armed volunteers who eventually bolstered the ranks of the reformed city guard.
Visored barbutes are in no way inferior to sallets and bascinets. Choosing one over another is a matter of preference.
Visored barbutes are in no way inferior to sallets and bascinets. Choosing one over another is a matter of preference.

High-born or not, some knights don't have a single coin to their name. Affording first-rate armor is out of the question, so is wearing a common soldier's kettle helmet. And that's where cervelliers come into play.

High-born or not, some knights don't have a single coin to their name. Affording first-rate armor is out of the question, so is wearing a common soldier's kettle helmet. And that's where cervelliers come into play.
One of the heaviest helmets out there, it can protect your head and neck from just about anything - not to mention how formidable it looks.
One of the heaviest helmets out there, it can protect your head and neck from just about anything - not to mention how formidable it looks.

Its glory years are long behind it. In this day and age, Aldorian armorsmiths are capable of forging much more efficient designs. And even though the visor adds an extra layer of protection, the whole thing is still outrageously outdated.
Fear makes the wolf bigger than he is, and it's hard to see a damn thing in one of these sallets - as a result, they are often worn by soldiers fighting on the front line.
Fear makes the wolf bigger than he is, and it's hard to see a damn thing in one of these sallets - as a result, they are often worn by soldiers fighting on the front line.
Complete with a full visor, sentinets offer protection on par with the more widespread sallets and barbutes. In some aspects, they even surpass them.
Complete with a full visor, sentinets offer protection on par with the more widespread sallets and barbutes. In some aspects, they even surpass them.

Noble Marrs, Zeineshes, and Chervenians take pride in adorning their clothing with traditional folk patterns, seizing every opportunity to assert their culture in blatant defiance of the ruling Ksifit minority.
Maces are held in great regard in Skadia, where even high-born military commanders don't scoff at using them on the battlefield - although this particular weapon looks somewhat ceremonial in nature.
A predecessor to halberds, a voulge is still occasionally used by guards and some militias.
Thickly woven and fitted with a quilted mantelet - the only source of comfort a wandering mage can truly rely on.
These cowls are usually pulled over one's eyes: many commoners believe that a mage's gaze brings woes and misfortune, so why not ease their fears?

There are mages who make a living by wandering the world, offering their services to anyone willing to pay. Most of them suffer a miserable fate: some die after taking on a contract they can't handle, some join brigand gangs, while others fall victim to the mob, which is quick to act upon its prejudice.
A step above the kettle helmet, tougher and more resistant to both melee and ranged weaponry.
There isn't a single agricultural tool which hasn't been repurposed for military use.
A weapon of uncertain times - a humble scythe blade, fastened to a wooden handle.
It's sharp to such a degree that a strong enough strike will easily separate your opponent's head from their body.
Hand imagery is considered a powerful ward in Aldor, capable of protecting its user from misfortune, sickness, and curses.
The material leaves much to be desired, but the craftsmanship shows no small measure of ingenuity.
Spend a few months actively using this crossbow, and even village fair strongmen will envy your muscles.
Just an old, dusty hat, but for some reason you are compelled to put it on your head.
In a witch's hands, it lays terrible hexes - and sustains her life by doing so.
A bascinet with a grated visor and a nose guard. Protects the face better than an open variant.
The Wolfbrothers are considered the elite of the Gray Army, even if the words "elite" and "Gray Army" seem to contradict each other.
Hefty enough to roughen up a training dummy.

The Woodwights were a band of Eeders rebels who gave the royal authorities a run for their money about a decade ago. Eventually, every Woodwight was captured and quartered, but the flails they used continue to live on.
While suppressing the Eeders' rebellion a dozen years ago, the nobles received an unexpected and rather unwelcome reminder: staff slings are far deadlier than one might assume.
For when you don't want to get your hands dirty.
Judging by the abundant patchwork, this cloak has been in use for many years.
Yew is generally used to make longbows, but sometimes craftsmen settle for something more modest.
